paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: In 1907, Leo Hendrick Baekeland, a Belgian scientist working in New York, discovered and patented a revolutionary new synthetic material. His invention, which he named 'Bakelite', was of enormous technological importance, and effectively launched the modern plastics industry.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: The term 'plastic' comes from the Greek plassein, meaning 'to mould'. Some plastics are derived from natural sources, some are semi-synthetic (the result of chemical action on a natural substance), and some are entirely synthetic, that is, chemically engineered from the constituents of coal or oil. Some are 'thermoplastic', which means that, like candlewax, they melt when heated and can then be reshaped. Others are 'thermosetting': like eggs, they cannot revert to their original viscous state, and their shape is thus fixed forever. Bakelite had the distinction of being the first totally synthetic thermosetting plastic.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: The history of today's plastics begins with the discovery of a series of semi-synthetic thermoplastic materials in the mid-nineteenth century. The impetus behind the development of these early plastics was generated by a number of factors - immense technological progress in the domain of chemistry, coupled with wider cultural changes, and the pragmatic need to find acceptable substitutes for dwindling supplies of 'luxury' materials such as tortoiseshell and ivory.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Baekeland's interest in plastics began in 1885 when, as a young chemistry student in Belgium, he embarked on research into phenolic resins, the group of sticky substances produced when phenol (carbolic acid) combines with an aldehyde (a volatile fluid similar to alcohol). He soon abandoned the subject, however, only returning to it some years later. By 1905 he was a wealthy New Yorker, having recently made his fortune with the invention of a new photographic paper. While Baekeland had been busily amassing dollars, some advances had been made in the development of plastics. The years 1899 and 1900 had seen the patenting of the first semi-synthetic thermosetting material that could be manufactured on an industrial scale. In purely scientific terms, Baekeland's major contribution to the field is not so much the actual discovery of the material to which he gave his name, but rather the method by which a reaction between phenol and formaldehyde could be controlled, thus making possible its preparation on a commercial basis. On 13 July 1907, Baekeland took out his famous patent describing this preparation, the essential features of which are still in use today.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: The original patent outlined a three-stage process, in which phenol and formaldehyde (from wood or coal) were initially combined under vacuum inside a large egg-shaped kettle. The result was a resin known as Novalak, which became soluble and malleable when heated. The resin was allowed to cool in shallow trays until it hardened, and then broken up and ground into powder. Other substances were then introduced: including fillers, such as wood flour, asbestos or cotton, which increase strength and moisture resistance, catalysts (substances to speed up the reaction between two chemicals without joining to either) and hexa, a compound of ammonia and formaldehyde which supplied the additional formaldehyde necessary to form a thermosetting resin. This resin was then left to cool and harden, and ground up a second time. The resulting granular powder was raw Bakelite, ready to be made into a vast range of manufactured objects. In the last stage, the heated Bakelite was poured into a hollow mould of the required shape and subjected to extreme heat and pressure, thereby 'setting' its form for life.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: The design of Bakelite objects, everything from earrings to television sets, was governed to a large extent by the technical requirements of the moulding process. The object could not be designed so that it was locked into the mould and therefore difficult to extract. A common general rule was that objects should taper towards the deepest part of the mould, and if necessary the product was moulded in separate pieces. Moulds had to be carefully designed so that the molten Bakelite would flow evenly and completely into the mould. Sharp corners proved impractical and were thus avoided, giving rise to the smooth, 'streamlined' style popular in the 1930s. The thickness of the walls of the mould was also crucial: thick walls took longer to cool and harden, a factor which had to be considered by the designer in order to make the most efficient use of machines.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: Baekeland's invention, although treated with disdain in its early years, went on to enjoy an unparalleled popularity which lasted throughout the first half of the twentieth century. It became the wonder product of the new world of industrial expansion - 'the material of a thousand uses'. Being both non-porous and heat-resistant, Bakelite kitchen goods were promoted as being germ-free and sterilisable. Electrical manufacturers seized on its insulating properties, and consumers everywhere relished its dazzling array of shades, delighted that they were now, at last, no longer restricted to the wood tones and drab browns of the pre-plastic era. It then fell from favour again during the 1950s, and was despised and destroyed in vast quantities. Recently, however, it has been experiencing something of a renaissance, with renewed demand for original Bakelite objects in the collectors' marketplace, and museums, societies and dedicated individuals once again appreciating the style and originality of this innovative material.

groups.desc:
Questions 1-3
Complete the summary.
Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the text ineach gap.
groups.collect:
Some plastics behave in a similar way to$2066$in that they melt under heat and can be moulded into new forms. Bakelite was unique because it was the first material to be both entirely$2067$in origin, and thermosetting.
There were several reasons for the research into plastics in the nineteenth century, among them the great advances that had been made in the field of $2068$and the search for alternatives to natural resources like ivory.

questions.analysis: 题干中的“behave in a similar way to”对应原文中的“like”，“melt under heat”对应“melt when heated”，“moulded into new forms”对应“reshaped”。这些同义表达帮助我们定位到答案句。答案句中明确提到“like candle wax”，说明蜡烛蜡在加热时会熔化并可以重新塑形，这与题干描述的塑料行为一致。因此，正确答案是“candle wax”。

central_sentences.sentence: Some are 'thermoplastic', which means that, like candle wax, they melt when heated and can then be reshaped.
central_sentences.translation: 有些是"热塑性"的，这意味着它们像蜡烛蜡一样，加热时会熔化，然后可以重新塑形。

target_sen.target_sen: Some are 'thermoplastic', which means that, like candle wax, they melt when heated and can then be reshaped.
Some are 'thermoplastic', which means that, like candle wax, they melt when heated and can then be reshaped.

questions.analysis: 答案是 "synthetic"。题干中的 "entirely" 对应文章中的 "totally"，"in origin" 对应 "synthetic"，"thermosetting" 在题干和文章中都直接出现。通过这些对应关系，我们可以确定答案是 "synthetic"。这个答案是正确的，因为它准确描述了 Bakelite 的起源和性质。

central_sentences.sentence: Bakelite had the distinction of being the first totally synthetic thermosetting plastic.
central_sentences.translation: Bakelite具有独特之处，它是第一种完全合成的热固性塑料。

target_sen.target_sen: Bakelite had the distinction of being the first totally synthetic thermosetting plastic.
Bakelite had the distinction of being the first totally synthetic thermosetting plastic.
Others are 'thermosetting': like eggs, they cannot revert to their original viscous state, and their shape is thus fixed for ever.
Bakelite had the distinction of being the first totally synthetic thermosetting plastic.

questions.analysis: 答案是 "chemistry"。题干中的 "great advances" 对应文章中的 "immense technological progress"，"field of" 对应 "domain of"，"natural resources like ivory" 对应 "dwindling supplies of 'luxury' materials such as tortoiseshell and ivory"。通过这些同义表达，可以确认答案所在的句子是 "The impetus behind the development of these early plastics was generated by a number of factors - immense technological progress in the domain of chemistry, coupled with wider cultural changes, and the pragmatic need to find acceptable substitutes for dwindling supplies of 'luxury' materials such as tortoiseshell and ivory."。因此，答案是 "chemistry"。

central_sentences.sentence: The impetus behind the development of these early plastics was generated by a number of factors - immense technological progress in the domain of chemistry, coupled with wider cultural changes, and the pragmatic need to find acceptable substitutes for dwindling supplies of 'luxury' materials such as tortoiseshell and ivory.
central_sentences.translation: 推动这些早期塑料发展的动力源于多个因素 - 化学领域的巨大技术进步，加上更广泛的文化变革，以及寻找可接受的替代品来替代日益减少的"奢侈品"材料（如玳瑁和象牙）的实际需求。

groups.title: The Production of Bakelite
groups.desc:
Questions 4-8
Complete the flow-chart.
Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the text in each gap.

questions.analysis: 题干中的“stage one resin, called”要求我们找到第一阶段树脂的名称。文章中提到“a resin known as Novalak”，这与题干中的“stage one resin, called”直接对应。通过定位关键词“stage one resin”和“called”，我们可以在文章中找到对应的句子，明确答案是“Novalak”。学生需要注意的是，题干要求的是特定名称，而不是一般描述，因此不能错填“resin”或“phenol”。

central_sentences.sentence: The original patent outlined a three-stage process, in which phenol and formaldehyde (from wood or coal) were initially combined under vacuum inside a large egg-shaped kettle. The result was a resin known as Novalak, which became soluble and malleable when heated.
central_sentences.translation: 原始专利概述了一个三阶段的过程，其中苯酚和甲醛（来自木材或煤炭）最初在真空下在一个大蛋形水壶中结合。结果是一种称为Novalak的树脂，当加热时变得可溶和可塑。

target_sen.target_sen: The result was a resin known as Novalak, which became soluble and malleable when heated.
The result was a resin known as Novalak, which became soluble and malleable when heated.

questions.analysis: 题干中提到的“cotton, asbestos”在原文中对应的是“wood flour, asbestos or cotton”，而这些物质被称为“fillers”。因此，第5题的正确答案是“fillers”。

central_sentences.sentence: Other substances were then introduced: including fillers, such as wood flour, asbestos or cotton, which increase strength and moisture resistance, catalysts
central_sentences.translation: 然后引入了其他物质：包括填充物，如木粉、石棉或棉花，这些物质可以增加强度和防潮性...

target_sen.target_sen: Other substances were then introduced: including fillers, such as wood flour, asbestos or cotton, which increase strength and moisture resistance, catalysts (substances to speed up the reaction between two chemicals without joining to either) and hexa, a compound of ammonia and formaldehyde which supplied the additional formaldehyde necessary to form a thermosetting resin.

questions.analysis: 题干中提到“ammonia”和“formaldehyde”，我们需要找到与这两个词相关的内容。在原文中，明确提到“hexa, a compound of ammonia and formaldehyde”，这与题干中的描述完全对应。因此，答案是“hexa”。学生需要注意的是，虽然同一段落中提到了其他物质如“catalysts”和“fillers”，但它们并不是由“ammonia”和“formaldehyde”组成的，因此不能作为答案。

central_sentences.sentence: Other substances were then introduced: including fillers, such as wood flour, asbestos or cotton, which increase strength and moisture resistance, catalysts (substances to speed up the reaction between two chemicals without joining to either) and hexa, a compound of ammonia and formaldehyde which supplied the additional formaldehyde necessary to form a thermosetting resin.
central_sentences.translation: 然后引入了其他物质：包括填料，如木粉、石棉或棉花，这些填料增加了强度和耐湿性，催化剂（加速两种化学物质之间反应但不与任何一种结合的物质）和hexa，这是一种由氨和甲醛组成的化合物，提供了形成热固性树脂所需的额外甲醛。

target_sen.target_sen: Other substances were then introduced: including fillers, such as wood flour, asbestos or cotton, which increase strength and moisture resistance, catalysts (substances to speed up the reaction between two chemicals without joining to either) and hexa, a compound of ammonia and formaldehyde which supplied the additional formaldehyde necessary to form a thermosetting resin.
The original patent outlined a three-stage process, in which phenol and formaldehyde (from wood or coal) were initially combined under vacuum inside a large egg-shaped kettle.

Other substances were then introduced: including fillers, such as wood flour, asbestos or cotton, which increase strength and moisture resistance, catalysts (substances to speed up the reaction between two chemicals without joining to either) and hexa, a compound of ammonia and formaldehyde which supplied the additional formaldehyde necessary to form a thermosetting resin.

questions.analysis: 题干中的 "break up and grind into powder" 对应文章中的 "break up and ground into powder"，而 "Bakelite" 对应 "raw Bakelite"。通过这些对应关系，可以定位到文章中的句子 "The resulting granular powder was raw Bakelite, ready to be made into a vast range of manufactured objects." 这句话明确指出了粉末状态的电木是 "raw Bakelite"，因此答案是 "raw"。学生需要注意的是，题目要求的是形容电木的词语，而不是描述粉末的词语。

central_sentences.sentence: The resulting granular powder was raw Bakelite, ready to be made into a vast range of manufactured objects.
central_sentences.translation: 生成的颗粒状粉末是原始电木，准备制成各种各样的制成品。

syn_ary.synonym: break up and ground into powder

target_sen.target_sen: The resin was allowed to cool in shallow trays until it hardened, and then broken up and ground into powder.

questions.analysis: 题干中的 "apply intense heat" 在原文中对应 "subjected to extreme heat"，并且 "and" 连接的内容在原文中是 "pressure"。因此，答案是 "pressure"。学生需要注意并列结构中的内容，理解并找到与题干描述相对应的同义表达。

central_sentences.sentence: In the last stage, the heated Bakelite was poured into a hollow mould of the required shape and subjected to extreme heat and pressure, thereby 'setting' its form for life.
central_sentences.translation: 在最后阶段，加热后的电木被倒入所需形状的空心模具中，并受到极端的热和压力，从而使其形状固定。

target_sen.target_sen: In the last stage, the heated Bakelite was poured into a hollow mould of the required shape and subjected to extreme heat and pressure, thereby 'setting' its form for life.

The result was a resin known as Novalak, which became soluble and malleable when heated.
The resin was allowed to cool in shallow trays until it hardened, and then broken up and ground into powder.

Other substances were then introduced: including fillers, such as wood flour, asbestos or cotton, which increase strength and moisture resistance, catalysts (substances to speed up the reaction between two chemicals without joining to either) and hexa, a compound of ammonia and formaldehyde which supplied the additional formaldehyde necessary to form a thermosetting resin.
This resin was then left to cool and harden, and ground up a second time.

groups.desc:
Questions 11-13

Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1?
Choose
TRUE if the statement agrees with the information,
FALSE if the statement contradicts the information,
NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this.

questions.title: A Modern-day plastic preparation is based on the same principles as that patented in 1907.
questions.analysis: 这道题的正确答案是 TRUE。题干中的 "Modern-day plastic preparation is based on the same principles as that patented in 1907" 意思是现代的塑料制备方法基于1907年获得专利的相同原理。原文中提到 "On 13 July 1907, Baekeland took out his famous patent describing this preparation, the essential features of which are still in use today." 这句话明确表示1907年贝克兰德获得专利的制备方法的基本特征至今仍在使用。因此，题干中的信息在文章中得到了验证，答案为 TRUE。

central_sentences.sentence: On 13 July 1907, Baekeland took out his famous patent describing this preparation, the essential features of which are still in use today.
central_sentences.translation: 1907年7月13日，贝克兰获得了他著名的专利，描述了这种制备方法，其基本特征至今仍在使用。

syn_ary.synonym: the essential features of which are still in use today

target_sen.target_sen: On 13 July 1907, Baekeland took out his famous patent describing this preparation, the essential features of which are still in use today.

questions.title: Bakelite was immediately welcomed as a practical and versatile material.
questions.analysis: 题干中的“immediately welcomed”与原文中的“treated with disdain”形成了直接的对比，表明Bakelite在早期并没有被立即欢迎。题干中的“practical and versatile material”与原文中的“unparalleled popularity”相对应，但这并不改变它在早期被鄙视的事实。因此，正确答案是FALSE。

central_sentences.sentence: Baekeland's invention, although treated with disdain in its early years, went on to enjoy an unparalleled popularity which lasted throughout the first half of the twentieth century.
central_sentences.translation: 虽然在早期被鄙视，但贝克兰的发明后来在整个二十世纪上半叶享有无与伦比的欢迎度。

syn_ary.synonym: treated with disdain in its early years

target_sen.target_sen: Baekeland's invention, although treated with disdain in its early years, went on to enjoy an unparalleled popularity which lasted throughout the first half of the twentieth century.

syn_ary.synonym: the material of a thousand uses

target_sen.target_sen: It became the wonder product of the new world of industrial expansion - 'the material of a thousand uses'.

questions.title: Bakelite was only available in a limited range of colours.
questions.analysis: 题干中的“limited range of colours”意思是“颜色范围有限”，而原文中的“dazzling array of shades”意思是“令人眼花缭乱的色调”，这表明Bakelite有很多种颜色。因此，题干中的信息与原文内容相反，所以答案是FALSE。

central_sentences.sentence: consumers everywhere relished its dazzling array of shades, delighted that they were now, at last, no longer restricted to the wood tones and drab browns of the pre-plastic era.
central_sentences.translation: 消费者们到处都喜爱它令人眼花缭乱的各种色调，他们欣喜若狂，因为他们终于不再局限于塑料时代之前的木质色调和单调的棕色了。

target_sen.target_sen: Electrical manufacturers seized on its insulating properties, and consumers everywhere relished its dazzling array of shades, delighted that they were now, at last, no longer restricted to the wood tones and drab browns of the pre-plastic era.

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: The joke comes over the headphones: 'Which side of a dog has the most hair? The left.' No, not funny. Try again. 'Which side of a dog has the most hair? The outside.' Hah! The punchline is silly yet fitting, tempting a smile, even a laugh. Laughter has always struck people as deeply mysterious, perhaps pointless. The writer Arthur Koestler dubbed it the luxury reflex: 'unique in that it serves no apparent biological purpose'.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Theories about humour have an ancient pedigree. Plato expressed the idea that humour is simply a delighted feeling of superiority over others. Kant and Freud felt that joke-telling relies on building up a psychic tension which is safely punctured by the ludicrousness of the punchline. But most modern humour theorists have settled on some version of Aristotle's belief that jokes are based on a reaction to or resolution of incongruity, when the punchline is either a nonsense or, though appearing silly, has a clever second meaning.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Graeme Ritchie, a computational linguist in Edinburgh, studies the linguistic structure of jokes in order to understand not only humour but language understanding and reasoning in machines. He says that while there is no single format for jokes, many revolve around a sudden and surprising conceptual shift. A comedian will present a situation followed by an unexpected interpretation that is also apt.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: So even if a punchline sounds silly, the listener can see there is a clever semantic fit and that sudden mental 'Aha!' is the buzz that makes us laugh. Viewed from this angle, humour is just a form of creative insight, a sudden leap to a new perspective.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: However, there is another type of laughter, the laughter of social appeasement and it is important to understand this too. Play is a crucial part of development in most young mammals. Rats produce ultrasonic squeaks to prevent their scuffles turning nasty. Chimpanzees have a 'play-face' - a gaping expression accompanied by a panting 'ah, ah' noise. In humans, these signals have mutated into smiles and laughs. Researchers believe social situations, rather than cognitive events such as jokes, trigger these instinctual markers of play or appeasement. People laugh on fairground rides or when tickled to flag a play situation, whether they feel amused or not.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Both social and cognitive types of laughter tap into the same expressive machinery in our brains, the emotion and motor circuits that produce smiles and excited vocalisations. However, if cognitive laughter is the product of more general thought processes, it should result from more expansive brain activity.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: Psychologist Vinod Goel investigated humour using the new technique of 'single event' functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI). An MRI scanner uses magnetic fields and radio waves to track the changes in oxygenated blood that accompany mental activity. Until recently, MRI scanners needed several minutes of activity and so could not be used to track rapid thought processes such as comprehending a joke. New developments now allow half-second 'snapshots' of all sorts of reasoning and problem-solving activities.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: Although Goel felt being inside a brain scanner was hardly the ideal place for appreciating a joke, he found evidence that understanding a joke involves a widespread mental shift. His scans showed that at the beginning of a joke the listener's prefrontal cortex lit up, particularly the right prefrontal believed to be critical for problem solving. But there was also activity in the temporal lobes at the side of the head (consistent with attempts to rouse stored knowledge) and in many other brain areas. Then when the punchline arrived, a new area sprang to life - the orbital prefrontal cortex. This patch of brain tucked behind the orbits of the eyes is associated with evaluating information.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: Making a rapid emotional assessment of the events of the moment is an extremely demanding job for the brain, animal or human. Energy and arousal levels may need to be retuned in the blink of an eye. These abrupt changes will produce either positive or negative feelings. The orbital cortex, the region that becomes active in Goel's experiment, seems the best candidate for the site that feeds such feelings into higher-level thought processes, with its close connections to the brain's sub-cortical arousal apparatus and centres of metabolic control.

paragraphs.label: J
paragraphs.text: All warm-blooded animals make constant tiny adjustments in arousal in response to external events, but humans, who have developed a much more complicated internal life as a result of language, respond emotionally not only to their surroundings, but to their own thoughts. Whenever a sought-for answer snaps into place, there is a shudder of pleased recognition. Creative discovery being pleasurable, humans have learned to find ways of milking this natural response. The fact that jokes tap into our general evaluative machinery explains why the line between funny and disgusting, or funny and frightening, can be so fine. Whether a joke gives pleasure or pain depends on a person's outlook.

paragraphs.label: K
paragraphs.text: Humour may be a luxury, but the mechanism behind it is no evolutionary accident. As Peter Derks, a psychologist at William and Mary College in Virginia, says: 'I like to think of humour as the distorted mirror of the mind. It's creative, perceptual, analytical and lingual. If we can figure out how the mind processes humour, then we'll have a pretty good handle on how it works in general.'

questions.title: Arthur Koestler considered laughter biologically important in several ways.
questions.analysis: 题干中的“Arthur Koestler considered laughter biologically important in several ways”意思是“阿瑟·科斯特勒认为笑在生物学上有多种重要性”。而原文中提到“Arthur Koestler”时，明确指出他认为笑“没有明显的生物学目的”（no apparent biological purpose）。这与题干的陈述是相反的，因此答案是FALSE。

central_sentences.sentence: The writer Arthur Koestler dubbed it the luxury reflex: 'unique in that it serves no apparent biological purpose'.
central_sentences.translation: 作家阿瑟·柯斯勒将其称为奢侈反射：'独特之处在于它没有明显的生物学目的'。

questions.title: Plato believed humour to be a sign of above-average intelligence.
questions.analysis: 题干中的信息是“Plato believed humour to be a sign of above-average intelligence.”，而原文中提到的是“Plato expressed the idea that humour is simply a delighted feeling of superiority over others.”。题干中的“above-average intelligence”与原文中的“superiority over others”并不完全对应。柏拉图认为幽默是优越感的表现，而不是智力的表现。因此，题干中的信息在文章中并没有明确提到，所以答案是 NOT GIVEN。

central_sentences.sentence: Plato expressed the idea that humour is simply a delighted feeling of superiority over others.
central_sentences.translation: 柏拉图表达了这样一种想法，即幽默仅仅是一种对他人优越感的愉悦感觉。

target_sen.target_sen: Plato expressed the idea that humour is simply a delighted feeling of superiority over others.
Theories about humour have an ancient pedigree.
Plato expressed the idea that humour is simply a delighted feeling of superiority over others.

But most modern humour theorists have settled on some version of Aristotle's belief that jokes are based on a reaction to or resolution of incongruity, when the punchline is either a nonsense or, though appearing silly, has a clever second meaning.

questions.title: Kant believed that a successful joke involves the controlled release of nervous energy.
questions.analysis: 题干中的“Kant believed that a successful joke involves the controlled release of nervous energy”与原文中的“Kant and Freud felt that joke-telling relies on building up a psychic tension which is safely punctured by the ludicrousness of the punchline”表达的意思相同。题干中的“controlled release of nervous energy”对应原文中的“safely punctured by the ludicrousness of the punchline”，因此答案为TRUE。

questions.title: Current thinking on humour has largely ignored Aristotle's view on the subject.

central_sentences.sentence: But most modern humour theorists have settled on some version of Aristotle's belief that jokes are based on a reaction to or resolution of incongruity, when the punchline is either a nonsense or, though appearing silly, has a clever second meaning.

content.title: The Birth of Scientific English
content.notes: ['1 lingua franca: a language which is used for communication between groups of people who speak different languages']

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: World science is dominated today by a small number of languages, including Japanese, German and French, but it is English which is probably the most popular global language of science. This is not just because of the importance of English-speaking countries such as the USA in scientific research; the scientists of many non-English-speaking countries find that they need to write their research papers in English to reach a wide international audience. Given the prominence of scientific English today, it may seem surprising that no one really knew how to write science in English before the 17th century. Before that, Latin was regarded as the lingua franca1 for European intellectuals.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: The European Renaissance (c. 14th-16th century) is sometimes called the 'revival of learning', a time of renewed interest in the 'lost knowledge'of classical times. At the same time, however, scholars also began to test and extend this knowledge. The emergent nation states of Europe developed competitive interests in world exploration and the development of trade. Such expansion, which was to take the English language west to America and east to India, was supported by scientific developments such as the discovery of magnetism (and hence the invention of the compass), improvements in cartography and - perhaps the most important scientific revolution of them all - the new theories of astronomy and the movement of the Earth in relation to the planets and stars, developed by Copernicus (1473-1543).

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: England was one of the first countries where scientists adopted and publicised Copernican ideas with enthusiasm. Some of these scholars, including two with interests in language -John Wallis and John Wilkins - helped found the Royal Society in 1660 in order to promote empirical scientific research.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Across Europe similar academies and societies arose, creating new national traditions of science. In the initial stages of the scientific revolution, most publications in the national languages were popular works, encyclopaedias, educational textbooks and translations. Original science was not done in English until the second half of the 17th century. For example, Newton published his mathematical treatise, known as the Principia, in Latin, but published his later work on the properties of light - Opticks - in English.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: There were several reasons why original science continued to be written in Latin. The first was simply a matter of audience. Latin was suitable for an international audience of scholars, whereas English reached a socially wider, but more local, audience. Hence, popular science was written in English.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: A second reason for writing in Latin may, perversely, have been a concern for secrecy. Open publication had dangers in putting into the public domain preliminary ideas which had not yet been fully exploited by their 'author'. This growing concern about intellectual property rights was a feature of the period - it reflected both the humanist notion of the individual, rational scientist who invents and discovers through private intellectual labour, and the growing connection between original science and commercial exploitation. There was something of a social distinction between 'scholars and gentlemen' who understood Latin, and men of trade who lacked a classical education. And in the mid-17th century it was common practice for mathematicians to keep their discoveries and proofs secret, by writing them in cipher, in obscure languages, or in private messages deposited in a sealed box with the Royal Society. Some scientists might have felt more comfortable with Latin precisely because its audience, though international, was socially restricted. Doctors clung the most keenly to Latin as an 'insider language'.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: A third reason why the writing of original science in English was delayed may have been to do with the linguistic inadequacy of English in the early modern period. English was not well equipped to deal with scientific argument. First, it lacked the necessary technical vocabulary. Second, it lacked the grammatical resources required to represent the world in an objective and impersonal way, and to discuss the relations, such as cause and effect, that might hold between complex and hypothetical entities.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: Fortunately, several members of the Royal Society possessed an interest in language and became engaged in various linguistic projects. Although a proposal in 1664 to establish a committee for improving the English language came to little, the society's members did a great deal to foster the publication of science in English and to encourage the development of a suitable writing style. Many members of the Royal Society also published monographs in English. One of the first was by Robert Hooke, the society's first curator of experiments, who described his experiments with microscopes in Micrographia (1665). This work is largely narrative in style, based on a transcript of oral demonstrations and lectures.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: In 1665 a new scientific journal, Philosophical Transactions, was inaugurated. Perhaps the first international English-language scientific journal, it encouraged a new genre of scientific writing, that of short, focused accounts of particular experiments.

paragraphs.label: J
paragraphs.text: The 17th century was thus a formative period in the establishment of scientific English. In the following century much of this momentum was lost as German established itself as the leading European language of science. It is estimated that by the end of the 18th century 401 German scientific journals had been established as opposed to 96 in France and 50 in England. However, in the 19th century scientific English again enjoyed substantial lexical growth as the industrial revolution created the need for new technical vocabulary, and new, specialised, professional societies were instituted to promote and publish in the new disciplines.

groups.desc:
Questions 28-34
Complete the summary.
Choose NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS from the text in each gap.
groups.collect:
In Europe, modern science emerged at the same time as the nation state. At first, the scientific language of choice remained$2085$. It allowed scientists to communicate with other socially privileged thinkers while protecting their work from unwanted exploitation. Sometimes the desire to protect ideas seems to have been stronger than the desire to communicate them, particularly in the case of mathematicians and$2086$.
In Britain, moreover, scientists worried that English had neither the$2087$nor the$2088$to express their ideas. This situation only changed after 1660 when scientists associated with the$2089$ set about developing English. An early scientific journal fostered a new kind of writing based on short descriptions of specific experiments. Although English was then overtaken by$2090$, it developed again in the 19th century as a direct result of the$2091$

questions.analysis: 题干中的“at first”对应原文中的“in the initial stages”，“scientific language”对应“original science”，“remained”对应“continued to be”。原文明确指出最初的科学语言是拉丁语，因此答案是“Latin”。

central_sentences.sentence: There were several reasons why original science continued to be written in Latin.
central_sentences.translation: 原创科学继续用拉丁语书写有几个原因。

target_sen.target_sen: There were several reasons why original science continued to be written in Latin.

questions.analysis: 题干中的 "protect ideas" 对应文章中的 "keep their discoveries and proofs secret"，"mathematicians" 在文章中直接提到，"particularly in the case of" 对应文章中的 "clung the most keenly to"。因此，答案是 "doctors"，因为文章明确指出医生尤其依赖拉丁语作为“内部语言”，以保护他们的想法。

central_sentences.sentence: Doctors clung the most keenly to Latin as an 'insider language'.
central_sentences.translation: 医生最热衷于将拉丁语作为一种"内部语言"。

target_sen.target_sen: And in the mid-17th century it was common practice for mathematicians to keep their discoveries and proofs secret, by writing them in cipher, in obscure languages, or in private messages deposited in a sealed box with the Royal Society.

And in the mid-17th century it was common practice for mathematicians to keep their discoveries and proofs secret, by writing them in cipher, in obscure languages, or in private messages deposited in a sealed box with the Royal Society.

questions.analysis: 题干中的“worried”对应原文中的“was not well equipped”，而“to express their ideas”对应原文中的“to deal with scientific argument”。题干中的“neither...nor...”结构提示了两个缺乏的方面，原文中列举了两个缺乏的方面时使用了“First,... Second,...”，这是重要的结构性提示。通过这些对应关系，我们可以确定答案为“technical vocabulary”和“grammatical resources”，因为这两个词是文章中明确指出的科学家担心英语缺乏的两方面。

central_sentences.sentence: English was not well equipped to deal with scientific argument. First, it lacked the necessary technical vocabulary. Second, it lacked the grammatical resources required to represent the world in an objective and impersonal way, and to discuss the relations, such as cause and effect, that might hold between complex and hypothetical entities.
central_sentences.translation: 英语不足以应对科学论证。首先，它缺乏必要的技术词汇。其次，它缺乏以客观和非个人化方式表述世界的语法资源，以及讨论复杂和假设实体之间可能存在的因果关系等关系的能力。

target_sen.target_sen: English was not well equipped to deal with scientific argument.
English was not well equipped to deal with scientific argument.

questions.analysis: 答案是 "grammatical resources" 和 "technical vocabulary"。题干中的 "neither...nor..." 结构提示我们需要两个名词。原文中明确提到 "it lacked the necessary technical vocabulary" 和 "it lacked the grammatical resources"，这两个短语直接对应题干中的空格部分。通过识别这些关键词和结构，能够准确找到答案。

central_sentences.sentence: English was not well equipped to deal with scientific argument. First, it lacked the necessary technical vocabulary. Second, it lacked the grammatical resources required to represent the world in an objective and impersonal way, and to discuss the relations, such as cause and effect, that might hold between complex and hypothetical entities.
central_sentences.translation: 英语并不适合处理科学论证。首先，它缺乏必要的技术词汇。其次，它缺乏以客观和非人称方式表达世界的语法资源，以及讨论复杂和假设实体之间可能存在的因果关系等关系的能力。

target_sen.target_sen: A third reason why the writing of original science in English was delayed may have been to do with the linguistic inadequacy of English in the early modern period.
English was not well equipped to deal with scientific argument.

questions.analysis: 答案是 "Royal Society"。题干中的 "after 1660" 对应文章中的 "in 1660"，"scientists associated with" 对应 "scholars... helped found"，"set about developing English" 对应 "helped found the Royal Society... to promote empirical scientific research"。这些同义表达帮助我们定位到答案句，并确认答案是 "Royal Society"。

central_sentences.sentence: Some of these scholars, including two with interests in language -John Wallis and John Wilkins - helped found the Royal Society in 1660 in order to promote empirical scientific research
central_sentences.translation: 一些学者，包括对语言感兴趣的约翰·沃利斯和约翰·威尔金斯，在1660年帮助创立了皇家学会，目的是促进实证科学研究。

target_sen.target_sen: Some of these scholars, including two with interests in language -John Wallis and John Wilkins - helped found the Royal Society in 1660 in order to promote empirical scientific research.
Some of these scholars, including two with interests in language -John Wallis and John Wilkins - helped found the Royal Society in 1660 in order to promote empirical scientific research.
Some of these scholars, including two with interests in language -John Wallis and John Wilkins - helped found the Royal Society in 1660 in order to promote empirical scientific research.

questions.analysis: 答案是 "German"。题干中的 "overtaken by" 对应原文中的 "established itself as the leading"，而 "19th century" 在原文中也明确提到。原文句子表明在18世纪，德语成为主要的科学语言，因此 "German" 是正确答案。

central_sentences.sentence: In the following century much of this momentum was lost as German established itself as the leading European language of science.
central_sentences.translation: 在接下来的一个世纪里，英语科学文献的发展势头大多失去了，因为德语确立了自己作为欧洲主要科学语言的地位。

target_sen.target_sen: In the following century much of this momentum was lost as German established itself as the leading European language of science.

questions.analysis: 答案“industrial revolution”是正确的，因为题干中提到的“19th century”和“direct result of”在原文中对应的是“in the 19th century”和“created the need for”，而这些需求是由“industrial revolution”引起的。因此，答案句明确指出了工业革命是19世纪科学英语再次发展的直接原因。

central_sentences.sentence: However, in the 19th century scientific English again enjoyed substantial lexical growth as the industrial revolution created the need for new technical vocabulary, and new, specialised, professional societies were instituted to promote and publish in the new disciplines.
central_sentences.translation: 然而，在19世纪，由于工业革命创造了对新技术词汇的需求，科学英语再次经历了显著的词汇增长，同时新的专业学会也成立了，以促进和发表新学科的研究。

target_sen.target_sen: However, in the 19th century scientific English again enjoyed substantial lexical growth as the industrial revolution created the need for new technical vocabulary, and new, specialised, professional societies were instituted to promote and publish in the new disciplines.

However, in the 19th century scientific English again enjoyed substantial lexical growth as the industrial revolution created the need for new technical vocabulary, and new, specialised, professional societies were instituted to promote and publish in the new disciplines.
The 17th century was thus a formative period in the establishment of scientific English.
In the following century much of this momentum was lost as German established itself as the leading European language of science.
It is estimated that by the end of the 18th century 401 German scientific journals had been established as opposed to 96 in France and 50 in England.

However, in the 19th century scientific English again enjoyed substantial lexical growth as the industrial revolution created the need for new technical vocabulary, and new, specialised, professional societies were instituted to promote and publish in the new disciplines.

groups.desc:
Questions 35-37

Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 3?
Choose
TRUE if the statement agrees with the information,
FALSE if the statement contradicts the information,
NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this.

questions.title: There was strong competition between scientists in Renaissance Europe.
questions.analysis: 题干中的信息是 "There was strong competition between scientists in Renaissance Europe." 需要确认文章中是否有提到文艺复兴时期欧洲科学家之间存在激烈竞争。文章中提到的是国家之间的竞争和科学学会的兴起，但并没有明确提到科学家之间的竞争。因此，题干中的信息在文章中找不到直接对应的表达，正确答案是 NOT GIVEN。

central_sentences.sentence: The emergent nation states of Europe developed competitive interests in world exploration and the development of trade.
central_sentences.translation: 欧洲新兴的民族国家在世界探索和贸易发展方面产生了竞争性的利益。

target_sen.target_sen: The emergent nation states of Europe developed competitive interests in world exploration and the development of trade.
At the same time, however, scholars also began to test and extend this knowledge.
The European Renaissance (c. 14th-16th century) is sometimes called the 'revival of learning', a time of renewed interest in the 'lost knowledge'of classical times.

questions.title: The most important scientific development of the Renaissance period was the discovery of magnetism.
questions.analysis: 题干中的 "most important scientific development" 对应原文中的 "perhaps the most important scientific revolution"，而题干中的 "discovery of magnetism" 也在原文中提到。通过对比可以发现，原文明确指出 "perhaps the most important scientific revolution" 是 "the new theories of astronomy and the movement of the Earth in relation to the planets and stars"，而不是 "discovery of magnetism"。因此，题干中的陈述是错误的，正确答案是 FALSE。

central_sentences.sentence: Such expansion, which was to take the English language west to America and east to India, was supported by scientific developments such as the discovery of magnetism (and hence the invention of the compass), improvements in cartography and - perhaps the most important scientific revolution of them all - the new theories of astronomy and the movement of the Earth in relation to the planets and stars, developed by Copernicus (1473-1543).
central_sentences.translation: 这种扩张使英语传播到了美洲西部和印度东部，它得到了科学发展的支持，如磁学的发现（因此发明了指南针），制图技术的改进，以及也许是所有科学革命中最重要的一项——哥白尼（1473-1543）发展的关于天文学和地球与行星、恒星运动关系的新理论。

syn_ary.synonym: perhaps the most important scientific revolution

target_sen.target_sen: Such expansion, which was to take the English language west to America and east to India, was supported by scientific developments such as the discovery of magnetism (and hence the invention of the compass), improvements in cartography and - perhaps the most important scientific revolution of them all - the new theories of astronomy and the movement of the Earth in relation to the planets and stars, developed by Copernicus (1473-1543).
The European Renaissance (c. 14th-16th century) is sometimes called the 'revival of learning', a time of renewed interest in the 'lost knowledge'of classical times.

Such expansion, which was to take the English language west to America and east to India, was supported by scientific developments such as the discovery of magnetism (and hence the invention of the compass), improvements in cartography and - perhaps the most important scientific revolution of them all - the new theories of astronomy and the movement of the Earth in relation to the planets and stars, developed by Copernicus (1473-1543).

questions.title: In 17th-century Britain, leading thinkers combined their interest in science with an interest in how to express ideas.
questions.analysis: 这道题的正确答案是TRUE，因为题干中的信息在文章中得到了明确的对应。题干提到17世纪英国的主要思想家将他们对科学的兴趣与对如何表达思想的兴趣结合起来。文章中提到“Several members of the Royal Society possessed an interest in language and became engaged in various linguistic projects.”，这表明皇家学会的成员（即主要思想家）对语言（即如何表达思想）有兴趣，并参与了语言项目（即结合了他们的科学兴趣和表达思想的兴趣）。因此，题干中的信息在文章中得到了验证。

central_sentences.sentence: several members of the Royal Society possessed an interest in language and became engaged in various linguistic projects. Although
central_sentences.translation: 皇家学会的一些成员对语言有兴趣，并参与了各种语言项目。

target_sen.target_sen: Fortunately, several members of the Royal Society possessed an interest in language and became engaged in various linguistic projects.
Many members of the Royal Society also published monographs in English.
Fortunately, several members of the Royal Society possessed an interest in language and became engaged in various linguistic projects.

locating_words.locating_word: interest in how to express ideas

groups.title: Science written in the first half of the 17th century
groups.desc:
Questions 38-40
Complete the table.
Choose NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS from the text ineach gap.

table.title: Science written in the first half of the 17th century

questions.analysis: 答案是“popular”，因为文章明确指出“popular science was written in English”，即通俗科学是用英语写的。这句话直接回答了题干中关于“Type of science - English”的问题。通过定位关键词“English”和“Type of science”，我们可以找到对应的句子，并得出正确答案。

central_sentences.sentence: Hence, popular science was written in English.
central_sentences.translation: 因此，通俗科学是用英语写的。

target_sen.target_sen: Latin was suitable for an international audience of scholars, whereas English reached a socially wider, but more local, audience.
Hence, popular science was written in English.
Hence, popular science was written in English.

questions.answer: Principia / the Principia / Newton's Principia / mathematical treatise
questions.analysis: 答案是“Principia”或其同义表达（如“the Principia”或“Newton's Principia”），因为文章明确指出牛顿的《Principia》是用拉丁语写的。题干中的“Examples”和“Latin”在原文中分别对应“for example”和“in Latin”，通过这些线索词可以定位到答案句。其他可能的错误答案如“Opticks”或“Micrographia”都是用英语写的，不符合题干要求。

central_sentences.sentence: For example, Newton published his mathematical treatise, known as the Principia, in Latin, but published his later work on the properties of light - Opticks - in English.
central_sentences.translation: 例如，牛顿发表了他的数学论文，即著名的《自然哲学的数学原理》（Principia），是用拉丁语写的，但他后来关于光学性质的著作《光学》则是用英语发表的。

target_sen.target_sen: For example, Newton published his mathematical treatise, known as the Principia, in Latin, but published his later work on the properties of light - Opticks - in English.
For example, Newton published his mathematical treatise, known as the Principia, in Latin, but published his later work on the properties of light - Opticks - in English.

questions.analysis: 答案是“local/more local/local audience”，因为题干中的“Target audience”对应原文中的“audience”，“socially wider”在原文中直接出现，而“English”也在原文中直接出现。原文句子明确指出“English reached a socially wider, but more local, audience”，这与题干中的“【 】, but socially wider”完全匹配。因此，答案是“local/more local/local audience”。

central_sentences.sentence: Latin was suitable for an international audience of scholars, whereas English reached a socially wider, but more local, audience.
central_sentences.translation: 拉丁语适合国际学者群体，而英语则能接触到社会范围更广，但更加本地化的受众。

target_sen.target_sen: The first was simply a matter of audience.
Latin was suitable for an international audience of scholars, whereas English reached a socially wider, but more local, audience.
Latin was suitable for an international audience of scholars, whereas English reached a socially wider, but more local, audience.
Hence, popular science was written in English.
Latin was suitable for an international audience of scholars, whereas English reached a socially wider, but more local, audience.
